data.title: UiPath: Buying Agentic AI At A 40% Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Analyzing UiPath (PATH) and the root causes of its 30% decline in market value in 2026, we must not confuse volatility with the risk of a loss in the company’s fundamental value. The agency AI solutions sector has undergone a synchronized correction since my first recommendation, behind which lies a massive transformation of PATH. In 2026, agency AI resembles the "Wild West" era, where both IT giants like Microsoft (MSFT) and agile startups offering automation with direct code are entering the fray. In this context, PATH is pursuing a strategy of transitioning from a niche RPA player to a comprehensive AI solutions platform that enables the automation of organizations’ business processes.

In parallel with high market volatility, the quarterly report from PATH exceeded expectations, starting with revenue and net profit growth and ending with updated forecasts for the coming calendar year. Analysts estimate the RPA market at $28.3 billion, but the company's transition to the agent AI segment allows it to scale its business by dividing the total market pie of $116 billion. This article identifies factors demonstrating PATH’s investment appeal, starting with a huge 40% discount to the median multiples of direct competitors, through to new partnerships and growth in orders for AI-based products. Therefore, despite pressure from new competitors and increased market volatility, I am reaffirming my "Buy" rating.

Previous Theses

The thesis underlying my previous research on UiPath has been the investment attractiveness of UiPath as an asset with strategic potential stemming from its decision to transition to a platform-based business model featuring AI-powered solutions. The first positive effects for the company, though, will not be seen until Q1 2026 at the earliest, and so the stock is still highly sensitive to market volatility. A result, PATH's market value has declined by 22% from the time of the first recommendation, which is partly due to the overall market conditions, with a decline in investor demand for risky assets. Another important factor in this price decline is the entry of new competitors into the market for agent-based AI solutions, including cloud giants and promising startups. The fact that the market for agent-based AI solutions is under pressure because of the market situation is confirmed by the stock performance since the beginning of 2026 for the main companies that are direct competitors to PATH. Those assets show a similar stock price decline of around 30%.

UiPath’s AI Products and Platform Business Model

The competitive advantage of PATH begins with a strategic choice to develop and promote an intelligent platform based on RPA products, encompassing a wide range of agent-based AI services. This has resulted in the company’s transition from a traditional player in the RPA segment to a full-fledged competitor in the agent-based AI market. These developments could enable PATH to scale significantly in the future, since the RPA market is projected to reach $28.3 billion by the end of 2026, whereas the agent-based AI market, growing at an average annual rate of 19%, in 2026 will reach $116.6 billion, growing to $558 billion by 2035. Its products are targeted at the B2B segment, helping client organizations optimize business processes of any complexity by implementing agent-based AI.

Due to this transformation, PATH’s business model has shifted from a tool-based model to a platform-based one, allowing it to overcome the limitation of a restricted service offering. This platform-based business model enables the company to expand its services and make them more comprehensive, automatically increasing the average contract value per client. Additionally, the expansion of the product portfolio has included the integration of new AI technologies, serving as the foundation of the commercial offering for customers interested in developing their management infrastructure.

In transitioning to agent-based AI, PATH has formed strategic partnerships with the following IT giants.

PATH's Competitive Environment

Competition in PATH's market is intensifying, with players in the RPA segment (Automation Anywhere and SS&C Blue Prism) now joined by companies offering similar services in the agent-based AI solutions segment. As I noted earlier, some of the most aggressive new competitors include Pegasystems (PEGA), Appian (APPN), and Kofax. The first company has similar AI solutions integrated into RPA; the second is focused on end-to-end process automation using its proprietary AI platform; the third provides a unified automation platform using a combination of AI solutions and RPA.

But the competition doesn’t end there, as the fast-growing agency AI solutions market is giving rise to new products offered by various software companies. Despite its partnership with PATH, Microsoft is the most formidable competitor, since it offers similar services to companies that are deeply integrated into the Windows ecosystem. The advantage of MSFT is the almost zero cost of entry for M365/Azure users and deep integration with Copilot and Azure AI. As a result, MSFT offers a simpler licensing model that allows it to poach customers who previously used PATH’s services.

The other key competitor vying for market share in the business process automation sector is Automation Anywhere. This company focuses on Agent-based Process Automation through its IQ Bot solution, enabling effective handling of unstructured data. Looking ahead to 2026, the company is banking on openness to third-party AI agents, presenting itself as a cloud-oriented platform. One more competitor is ServiceNow (NOW), going through a similar business model transformation to transition to a full-fledged platform. Because there are a bunch of customers who already use this company’s services, it’s pretty easy for them to switch to the new products.

Additionally, the field of agency AI solutions is evolving rapidly, leading to the launch of new platforms. The first quarter of 2026, in particular, was marked by the launch of products from startups such as CrewAI, Dify, and Lyzard. Often, these solutions involve direct interaction between their AI agents via APIs and code, presenting a long-term risk to PATH. This is because such platforms can learn to solve tasks without relying on a visual interface. Consequently, UiPath, who decided to shift its competitive advantage toward a unified control center for all AI agents, has become a target of greater scrutiny in the market. Should PATH lose out to the competition, it will rapidly lose market share to players offering cheaper solutions, such as those offered via cloud services.

Key Highlights of the Quarterly Report for Q4 FY2026

The last quarterly report from PATH was positive, since the actual results exceeded analysts’ consensus estimates. EPS was $0.30, up $0.05 from the forecast, with revenue coming in $16.3 million higher ($481.11 million, or +13.5% compared to the same period a year earlier). While it was previously expected that the main effects of PATH’s transformation would be felt starting in Q1 2026, the company’s AI-based products have already increased ARR by $200 million. The number of customers with deals exceeding $100,000 increased by 26%, while 16 of the 20 largest deals closed in Q4 2025 included products based on agent-based AI solutions.

In addition, it presented an updated forecast for fiscal year 2027, which anticipates revenue in the range of $1.754–1.759 billion, up from the previous forecast of $1.74 billion. Projected revenue for the first quarter has also been raised in the forecasts from $393.36 million to $395–$400 million. Furthermore, the ARR as of April 30, 2026, and January 31, 2027, was expected to be in the ranges of $1.894–$1.899 billion and $2.051–$2.056 billion, accordingly.

PATH Valuation

In order to assess PATH’s investment appeal, forward values of key multiples (EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA) should be analyzed in comparison with other competitors MSFT, PEGA, APPN, and NOW.

Median values for the EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples are 4.60x and 14.55x, respectively. That gives corresponding discounts of 48.04% and 32.51%. Overall, we get a median discount of 40.27%. These results indicate that PATH shares are significantly undervalued based on key multiples, which supports the high assessment of the company’s investment appeal.

Risks for PATH

As of now, I think that, apart from the competitive environment, two other risks could impact the outlook for PATH’s stock.

The first is the risk associated with the successful integration of new AI products into PATH’s business strategy, because now the company’s financial results depend to a greater extent on the effectiveness of its strategic partnerships. Most of the effects of the transition to the new business model will not be felt until at least Q1 2026, meaning that the next earnings report may already provide initial answers to questions about how effective the new products in the agency AI space are.

Second, the first risk is exacerbated by macroeconomic conditions, the situation of which may deteriorate due to the current global instability. The geopolitical situation in the Middle East is affecting energy prices, which increases the likelihood of rising inflation. While inflation expectations aren’t showing aggressive growth, it’s possible the Fed might take preemptive action by ending monetary easing and shifting to a tightening phase. Such decisions would lead to continued selling by market participants, impacting the market value of all stock market assets, PATH included.

Conclusion

Despite the emergence of new risks and threats, I maintain my previous "Buy" rating. The company is at a turning point as it develops new software integrated with AI technologies. These new products, the foundation of the platform-based business model, have been actively distributed, which allows most of the major contracts to be secured through their sale. Ultimately, the management of PATH is targeting an increase in ARR to $2 billion, a figure that will reflect the robustness of the platform and the expansion of the customer portfolio. And the target operating margin has been adjusted from 20% to 30%, reflecting growing confidence in the efficient use of operating resources. Moreover, this technical correction in the stock price presents a more attractive opportunity to buy PATH at a lower price. That increases the potential for further growth in the company’s market capitalization, because there is a huge 40% discount relative to direct competitors based on EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples.

data.title: UiPath: With Meaningful ARR Growth, This Company Has True AI Tailwinds
data.description:
Is AI a net headwind or a tailwind? This seems to be the core question that is dogging the stock market this year, sparking the "SaaSpocalypse" that has wiped billions off the market cap of the software industry across companies both large and small. All in all, I continue to hold the belief that the SaaSpocalypse narrative is overblown, and investors should look to near-term quarterly results for hints whether certain software companies are disruptors or candidates for disruption. After all, customers of these software companies can vote with their feet, and if a certain platform is no longer compelling, customers can churn out, especially in this tough macroeconomy.

UiPath (PATH), which was in the business of workflow and business process automation long before AI became a buzzword, looks like a company enjoying tailwinds. Though its share price has contracted ~30% since the start of the year (largely along with other software companies), the company's fundamentals have continued to shine, with healthy net expansion rates and ARR growth.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on UiPath in December, when the stock was trading at $18 per share. Needless to say, the sharp crash in the stock since then has disappointed me, and my buy call was ill-timed (as I certainly did not predict the SaaSpocalypse at the time). That said, such a sharp correction in any stock merits a fresh look, and especially when I assess the strength of UiPath's latest results against its freshly lowered valuation, I find an incredibly attractive bargain play. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Here's the reality that we need to confront with UiPath. Since agentic AI took off and automation became the core focus of CIOs and CFOs, UiPath's product is no longer incredibly unique. But at the same time, companies wishing to deploy automation tools now have two distinct options: DIY and build their own vibe-coded, agentic AI tools, or deploy proven existing tools. The former may be more customizable and cheaper, but the latter delivers enterprise support and faster time to value. UiPath can back up its product with years of experience in automating corporate workflows, a wide ecosystem of third-party integrations (which DIY tools may fail to incorporate), and strong recognition from unbiased industry reviewers (Forrester, one of the leading software industry reviewers, named UiPath a leader in autonomous testing platforms).

And yet the stock market is essentially treating UiPath as if it's a prime candidate for AI disruption, even though the company's double-digit revenue growth, ARR expansion, and net revenue retention rates suggest this is all but true.

In the meantime, as we wait for the stock market to correct upward, we have a chance to buy UiPath at incredibly favorable valuations. At current share prices near $11, UiPath trades at a market cap of $5.85 billion. Netting off the $1.70 billion of cash against zero debt on the company's latest balance sheet (another tremendous reason to like this stock) gives us an enterprise value of $4.14 billion.

For FY27 (the year for UiPath ending in January 2027), the company has guided to revenue of $1.754-$1.759 billion, or 9% y/y growth. It's expecting a $415 million pro forma operating profit, or a 23.6% margin, representing 60bps of y/y expansion.

This positions UiPath at just 2.4x EV/FY27 revenue. For a company that can point to AI tailwinds, is close to executing at the "Rule of 40", and has a buildup in ARR as well as healthy net retention, this is quite a steal. We note, of course, that the entire software sector has seen a contraction in multiples, and yet when we stack up UiPath against other ~10% growth software companies, we still find UiPath is an outlier on the low end of the industry's revenue multiples.

We also note that the company is taking advantage of its ironclad balance sheet and low share prices: it recently announced a fresh $500 million buyback plan, worth ~9% of the company's current market cap and fully fundable from current cash reserves. With this refreshed buyback authorization, UiPath can make use of this cheap window to offset dilution:

Stay long here and use UiPath's sharp slide this year as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through UiPath's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

UiPath's revenue grew 14% y/y to $481.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $464.8 million (+10% y/y) by a meaningful four-point margin.

Importantly, we note, as shown in the chart below, that the company added a substantial amount of net-new ARR in the quarter. Total ARR grew by $71 million sequentially in the quarter, rising 11% y/y to $1.85 billion. We note that guidance for FY27 doesn't imply any deceleration in ARR growth, with year-end ARR expected at $2.05 billion (+11% y/y). The fact that UiPath's ARR continues to exceed annual revenue positions the company well for consistent, reliable top-line growth.

We note as well that dollar-based net retention clocked in at 107%, flat to Q3 and indicating that the average customer is spending 7% y/y more on the UiPath platform. Neither the strong net expansion trends nor healthy ARR adds suggest that UiPath is struggling to win and retain business in the AI era, despite the stock market's pessimistic reading on the company.

In fact, management continues to cite AI as a tailwind, with more and more customers attaching the company's agentic AI solutions to their subscription packages. Per CEO Daniel Dines' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

The most important story this quarter is the economic shift underway inside our installed base. Customers are not experimenting with AI, they are expanding their operating model on our platform.

AI product ARR, which includes agentic, IDP and Maestro, reached nearly $200 million this quarter, with strong growth fueled by agentic. But the number I keep coming back to is this, the number of customers above $100,000 in ARR who have bought AI products grew 25% year-over-year and they spend nearly 3x as much as those who have not. Additionally, 16 of our top 20 deals this quarter included AI products. All of this is clear evidence that agentic automation is becoming central to our largest customers' roadmaps.

Importantly, this AI growth is layering on top of a core unattended automation business that continues to grow. We are not seeing AI agents replacing deterministic unattended automation in production we are seeing customers extending their processes with AI.

UiPath also maintained healthy profitability. GAAP operating margins more than doubled y/y to 17%, while pro forma operating margins held firm at 31% (rising 6 points y/y on a full-year basis to 23%, with further margin improvements expected in FY27).

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that we need to keep in mind here. UiPath may have been the incumbent in the automation space, but as many software companies release their own agentic AI tools to address more function-specific use cases (such as Salesforce's Agentforce products that automate sales and marketing workflows), UiPath may lose share as a more generalist competitor. We're also wary of the tighter IT budget environment and the tough macro, which may slow down new deal activity: UiPath has issued fairly aggressive guidance calling for ~$200 million in net-new ARR adds this year.

That said, we think these risks are already priced into the stock at just ~2x revenue. Take advantage of this year's slide to buy this attractive stock at a discount.

data.title: How To Capitalize On The Volatility Spike And Iran Conflict For Portfolio Rebalancing
data.description:
In today’s market, the Iran conflict has created great investment opportunities. However, too many investors are reacting, trying to anticipate these confounding military developments and trade on what is highly unpredictable. That is an unwise strategy, which is really speculation. Business news and brokerages want trading activity, but short-term trading is a flawed strategy. We believe investors should be positioning investments and strategies with great long-term potential at today’s excellent prices.

We believe portfolio construction will benefit by the inclusion of emerging markets, commodities, precious metals, and weak dollar plays.

The below should help guide portfolio construction based on the value of historic analogues.

Market Cycles:

The following chart shows inflationary and deflationary cycles based on inflationary trend data. We believe different periods are defined by different fundamental factors. The 1970s are well known for soaring gold and silver prices, a massive oil bull market, and a horrific US stock market. Likewise, the 1999 to 2009 period, is another inflationary period where gold, oil and emerging markets – China in particular – produced massive bull markets and contained two severe equity bear markets. We believe we are entering an inflationary cycle like the 1970s and the 1999-2009 period, and today’s unfolding inflationary cycle will show itself through a new pink section in the chart below that will persist for several years.

The Dollar:

DoubleLine CEO Jeff Gundlach has been a believer in the commodity versus equity cycle theory since 2018. In the last year, he has stated that the new trigger in this investment cycle is a declining dollar. Our cycle work suggests that the dollar will be weak for years, just as we believe gold, oil, and emerging markets will be strong for the next decade. The chart below shows a bounce in the dollar consistent with the US-Israel attack on Iran. We believe this counter-trend rally in the dollar will reverse as we inevitably prevail in stabilizing Iran and the Middle East in the coming months.

The 20-year chart below shows the bullish longer-term trend in the dollar since 2011; however, the dollar has been in a downtrend since 2022, excepting this recent Iran conflict’s counter-trend rally.

The five-year chart below shows the bear market in the dollar, which began in 2022, experiencing a conflict-related countertrend rally.

Gold

The sixty-year chart below shows gold in a new bull market run, which broke out over $2000/ounce in 2022. Pierre Lassonde recently stated in a YouTube video (1:58 min.) that he expects gold to be at $17,250 by 2032, and JP Morgan has upped their price target recently to $8000/ounce. So, despite gold’s recent parabolic run, we believe that after a consolidation period, a further leg peaking around the $8,000/ounce to $17,250/ounce range is possible by 2032.

Gold versus the Dow Jones Industrial Index History:

Charts showing different asset classes can show bubble behavior clearly. The chart below shows peak metal prices relative to the S&P 500’s topping in 2011, when gold prices exceeded the Dow and S&P 500. This analogy suggests a final cycle peak will be when gold catches up to the S&P 500, like in 2011, and should occur several years in the future.

Oil

Oil and energy should see a meaningful tenfold lift in the years ahead, based historic analogues. While buying oil stocks today may be too late, oil and gas service stocks should benefit from repairs and construction in Venezuela, Kuwait, the Middle East, Ukraine, and Russia in the future.

Small Capitalization

During bubble peaks, we typically see small caps rally to a premium in a parabolic upside move. The chart below suggests we are early in a cycle move where small caps outperform the large cap growth and MAG 7 stocks. The chart below suggests a rotation away from MAG 7 and mega cap growth stocks to small-cap stocks, and this trend should last into the 2030s.

Value stocks

During an inflationary cycle, value stocks tend to outperform growth stocks.

The chart below suggests we are at or near the growth peak following a growth outperformance boom that began around 2010. We think value stocks should outperform for the next several years.

Equity Valuations

The CAPE Ratio:

The largest speculative asset class is equities. Today’s S&P CAPE ratio is 38.9 near all-time highs. Based on previous highs in 1901, 1929, 1966, 2000, the current market could be entering a protracted bear market.

The Buffett Indicator

This US stock market indicator shows that based on the Total Market Capitalization divided by the GDP, we can see the stock market is near historically high valuations.

The Buffett Indicator chart below shows market valuations against a standard deviation backdrop. Today, the stock market is 2 standard deviations above the market average at 64.2%. Statistically, the odds of a decline in the years ahead significantly outweigh the chances of a bull market continuation.

The chart below shows we are down 8.74% from the top, and when the S&P 500 breaks, this decline could easily drop another 10%.

The margin debt indicator is starting to decline and appears to mark the commencement of an S&P 500 market liquidation cycle that may last a year or more.

International Equity Investments are historically cheap relative to the US market and the S&P 500. The Hang Seng out of Hong Kong looks very inexpensive at 10.74. Emerging markets, because of their lack of liquidity, may offer great opportunities in uncertain times like today because macroeconomic or geopolitical events create deep value opportunities in certain Emerging Markets.

The table below from Franklin Templeton shows market leadership has been led by emerging markets, foreign markets, and the Russell 1000 Value on a YTD and a one-year basis. We recommend reducing US equity exposure by selling large cap growth and adding Foreign Stocks, Emerging Market Equities and US small cap value.

Bonds

We are bearish on US bonds and recommend buying foreign bonds or adding cash to portfolios with 40% bond allocations. Investment guru Jeffrey Gundlach paints a bearish view on long term-Treasuries, the dollar, and US equities. He sees few markets, aside from gold, foreign and emerging markets, doing well.

Gold Miners

Gold miners are fundamentally cheap; have just exited a 14-year bear market; and the gold mining cycle is in its early development following decades of capital expenditure under-investment.

Gold and gold miners tend to be negatively correlated to the S&P 500. Both offer a good return history and are inversely correlated to the S&P 500. Gold miners offer non-correlated alpha in a commodity supercycle. The US has a massive federal debt and deficit, and this creates demand for gold.

Crescat Capital describes it this way:

At the same time, it takes about 15 years on average to advance a mining project from discovery to production. The world needs more metals now for AI datacenters, electrification, new US manufacturing onshoring, and defense, but the pipeline of new economic discoveries and viable development projects is scarce. These are the primary reasons we believe the cycle has strong legs for an entire decade ahead and this is why we favor the small cap explorers at this stage of the cycle. These companies carry risk and should be held in a professionally managed diversified portfolio, such as through our funds, but offer significantly more upside than the large-cap mining indices and ETFs in our view.

“The large cap S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 indices finally appear to be just starting to break down from record valuations, as we would expect with the new geopolitical conflict and resulting likely inflation and higher interest rates, but gold, silver, and mining stocks have sold off significantly more than these indices since Israel and the US first struck Iran on February 28. We think that presents an incredible short-term buying opportunity for the undervalued and still uncrowded mining stocks, especially the premier small cap explorers that we favor in our portfolios. Meanwhile, we see the long-slowing momentum, and now breakdown from the 200-day moving as a potential sell trigger for late-cycle, overcrowded, and overvalued US large cap equity indices and mega cap tech stocks.

The 20-year chart below of the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), the Van Eck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), and the Van Eck Junior Gold Miner ETF (GDXJ) shows a lead-lag delay in the appreciation of gold mining stocks relative to gold itself. Following the 14-year bear market in gold, once gold began to rise, gold mining companies’ profits on a delayed basis began improving. As those incremental profits began to come in, additional projects could be financed and drive both incremental revenues and higher gross margins. We believe that gold miners still have further to run until the demand for gold is met by the available supply of gold.

Based on cycle work, we believe that peak gold is years out despite the recent parabolic move in the metal and miners.

Conclusion

Rather than try to time the market based on war news from the Iranian conflict, this letter has sought to identify trends and values in the market that can allow investors to pick up good investments or trends made cheap by this conflict.

We view the major asset classes of US equities and US Treasuries as losing their investment appeal, which is creating new investment capital for inexpensive assets and geographies. We advocate rotating into international stocks and emerging market stocks, which are cheap and benefit from the weaker dollar.

  • Due to the Inflationary Cycle, we believe commodities, energy, gold, precious metals, foreign, and emerging market stocks, and foreign bonds should be bought with proceeds from US stocks and US Treasuries.
  • The weaker dollar is driving these trends, but with the Iran conflict, there has been a counter-trend rally creating better prices in these investments.
  • Gold can go a lot further. Due to foreign central bank purchases and gold reentering the mainstream as a credible investment for financial planning, we see gold hitting $8000/ounce to $17,250/ounce by 2032.
  • Oil should also benefit from the inflationary cycle, the commodity supercycle, and the Iranian conflict.
  • Small capitalization stocks and value stocks are both out of favor and logical investments.

The US stock market is historically overvalued and starting to crack. The CAPE Ratio today is 38.9, near all-time highs. Likewise, the Buffett indicator shows US stock valuations are two standard deviations above average and likely to crack in the future.

With the 9.7% break in the market, history points to another 10% decline. Furthermore, with the margin indicator now declining, the market appears to have entered a liquidation phase that can last for months or years.

Combined with increased cash holdings, owning gold miners appears to be one of the best sources of non-correlated risk in portfolio construction.

There is great money to be made in these turbulent times, but you must patiently buy cheap, diversifying assets and hold for the long term. As Warren Buffett said, Wayne Gretzky's secret to his success, is that you must “skate to where the puck will be.”

data.title: AH Realty Trust: Strong Assets With A Complex History
data.description:
AH Realty Trust’s (AHRT) forward outlook is slow and steady growth which warrants a higher multiple than the 10X forward AFFO at which it is trading. I think the market is misunderstanding the quality of AHRT’s assets as their asset level performance has been obscured by a complicated history. Specifically, 2 black swan events hit AHRT particularly hard, resulting in lost AFFO/share. The market is reading the losses as operating weakness and pricing AHRT stock well below the value of its well-performing assets.

2X Black Swan

AHRT’s history is mixed with high levels of success followed by precipitous decline starting in 2020.

AH Realty Trust’s history goes well beyond the left side of the above chart. It was a private company for decades before going public. They, like many private companies, used high leverage to fund developments. It was high risk and high reward, but it consistently paid off as their developments were repeatedly delivered on schedule and on or under budget.

It resulted in a healthy growth CAGR as a private company and continued to work nicely as a public company until 2020.

Then the pandemic hit.

The COVID shutdown hit just about all REITs, but one of the challenges of high leverage, is that it amplifies sensitivity to external stimuli. The shutdown, which was otherwise a temporary shock, became a longer lasting hit as high leverage forced liquidating some retail assets.

These assets were performing well aside from the depths of the shutdown. At the time, however, there was no way of knowing how long the pandemic shutdown would last and the ticking clock of leverage made disposing of assets the most prudent move.

As the economy re-opened it was back to business as usual for AHRT. They commenced a new slate of developments, all of which were underwritten to the prevailing standards at the time. Spreads were normal, and many of their developments already had tenants lined up such as T Rowe Price.

These developments have since been delivered and, as usual for AHRT, have performed well at an asset level. The construction went smoothly and lease-up has been on-pace.

However, the entire swath of development was derailed by an unforeseen event.

2nd black swan event – the fastest rate hikes in modern history

The company formerly known as Armada Hoffler often funded its developments with floating rate debt with the intent to refinance upon stabilization. The basic idea is that one can get a cheaper rate on a mortgage attached to a complete and stabilized asset. Thus, it would usually be disadvantageous to execute permanent financing prior to stabilization.

So AHRT was financing 5%-7% cap rate developments with floating rate debt that at the time appeared to cost around 4%.

That is a healthy spread, but then interest rates blew up. The Fed Funds rate went from 0% at the lower bound to over 5% in just over a year.

Most floating rate debt is tied to SOFR (or LIBOR at the time) so interest expense shot up with it.

AHRT was able to put some interest rate hedges into place to at least delay the onset of higher interest rates, but in a longer-term sense the economics of the slate of development were ruined.

The aforementioned hedges have kept the cost of debt at 4.2% for now.

However, the remaining term on AHRT’s debt is dwindling.

Much of the cheap debt will soon have to be replaced at somewhere around 6.5%

Thus, what was going to be roughly 6.0% cap rates financed by 4% debt now look more like 6.0% cap rates funded by 6.5% debt.

That is the unfortunate reality of the fastest interest rate hikes in history happening right after AHRT locked in hundreds of millions of dollars of developments.

Caught between a rock and a high-interest rate place, AHRT devised a solution: It would sell its entire portfolio of multifamily assets and use most of the proceeds to pay off debt.

The $562 million sale is contracted at a mid 5s cap rate. It was not fire sale prices. They achieved full asset value.

It is dilutive in the sense that the ~5.5% cap rate sale is being used to pay off 4.2% weighted average cost of debt. It is just less dilutive than allowing that 4.2% debt to roll into 6.5% debt.

So that is how AHRT got to where it is now.

The market is punishing the stock heavily for the decrease in AFFO/share. Most of the time, when a REIT loses AFFO/share it is related to asset failure of some kind. Perhaps a tenant fails, or rent gets cut, or occupancy comes in weak.

That sort of failure is indicative of poor property quality and indeed would often warrant trading at a low AFFO multiple.

AHRT’s lost AFFO/share was entirely related to leverage.

The assets themselves have performed quite well.

As such, AHRT should trade at a normal AFFO multiple with respect to its assets and only trade at a discount due to high leverage.

Well, AHRT is not going to be high leverage for long.

Once the multifamily asset sale completes, AHRT will pay down hundreds of millions of debt and be at leverage of 5.5X to 6.5X EBITDA. This is in the normal and analyst commended range for a REIT so it will no longer be high leverage.

Asset quality and balance sheet health

AHRT’s forward asset portfolio is about 55% retail and 45% office. Both have been performing quite well with the following same store NOI growth in 4Q25.

Let’s dig a bit deeper into each.

AHRT’s retail portfolio is largely located in mixed use properties such as retail on the first floor of an apartment or office building.

This makes them more like an Acadia (AKR) than a Brixmor (BRX).

The advantage of mixed-use retail is that it is very durable. The surrounding real estate keeps a constant flow of foot traffic such that the retail itself does not have to generate its own foot traffic.

The disadvantage, at least in AHRT’s case, is that the mark-to-market is significantly smaller.

Traditional shopping center REITs have low current rents relative to market rental rates, so they are looking at roll-ups upon signing new leases around 30%. In contrast, AHRT’s retail roll-ups are more like the mid-teens.

Thus, I would rate AHRT’s retail portfolio as very durable but slower growth. Organic growth is likely around 1%-2% annually.

Office is where AHRT really shines despite it being a weak asset class more broadly. Office performed very poorly over the last 5 years with even the biggest and most respected office REITs struggling. Occupancy has been low since the pandemic which is forcing landlords to fight for tenants with rent cuts and free rent periods.

Rather than being located in gateway cities, AHRT owns the only trophy office assets in what are otherwise small markets. Their office looks like Town Center of Virginia Beach or the brand-new T. Rowe Price headquarters in Baltimore Harbor. With few or zero competing assets of similar caliber in their submarkets, the competitive dynamics are much more landlord favoring.

In stark contrast to the rest of the office sector, AHRT has consistently been able to roll rents UP. Occupancy sits at a healthy 96%.

Valuation

AH Realty Trust is still trading at the multiple of a high-risk company.

The aspect of AHRT’s business that made it so susceptible to black swan events was its high leverage. That is no longer the case.

Going forward, it has strong assets that are now sitting on a healthy balance sheet. I would value the company as a composite of:

  • Quality but low growth retail
  • Supreme quality office

Shopping center REITs trade at an average P/AFFO of about 17X. Some of that premium is due to the high mark-to-market opportunity. Since AHRT has a smaller mark-to-market, I would spot fair value of its retail at about 15X AFFO.

Office REIT multiples are wide ranging and many have different flavors of properties.

As discussed earlier, AHRT’s office is significantly different and largely better in terms of operating performance. I would be inclined to value it independently from the sector.

AHRT’s office has moderate organic growth offset by somewhat high TI load (tenant improvement expenses). I think 12X would be a fair value multiple.

So between the retail and office I would spot AHRT’s overall fair value at 13.5X AFFO.

With clean forward AFFO of $0.54 (guided 59 cents minus stock comp addback) that would indicate a fair value of $7.29.

NAV Valuation

Wall Street analysts spot consensus NAV at $11.50.

This number is horribly outdated.

Many of the estimates still include NOI from the multifamily portfolio as well as one-time revenues that occurred in 2025.

We did our own NAV breakdown using a range of cap rates.

I think 7% is the fairest cap rate to use here. The retail assets could easily sell at a 6.5% cap rate and the office would be closer to 7.5%.

This spots AHRT’s NAV at about $8.00

Use of proceeds from the multifamily sale

The announced plans for how to spend the $562 million proceeds from the multifamily sale are some combination of:

  • Debt paydown
  • Share buybacks
  • Acquisitions of retail and/or office assets

The debt paydown is an excellent bucket as it both improves leverage ratios and prevents having to refinance debt at higher rates.

Share buybacks are highly accretive given the discount to NAV and low AFFO multiple at which AHRT trades.

As an investor I would want AHRT to allocate as much of the proceeds as possible to these 2 buckets with minimal acquisitions. They may have to allocate some to acquisitions for tax reasons (1031 exchange).

Management has shown a willingness to engage in significant share buybacks. Roughly a third of the proceeds from their real estate finance asset sale announced in late March were allocated to share buybacks.

The Bottom Line

As balance sheet improvement gets absorbed by the market I anticipate the stock to rerate to a higher AFFO multiple. Until then, AHRT represents an opportunity to buy high quality assets at a steep discount to NAV.

data.title: This Reliable Indicator Signals An Imminent Recession
data.description:
The entire world is worried about the energy shortage provoked by the Middle East conflict, and our investment portfolios are starting to feel some pain. Even though we are not in a technical bear market (at least a 20% drop from all time high), the main stock market indexes have crossed the -10% threshold and the recent news doesn’t bode well. How deep will we fall?

An interesting indicator that few people know about

The Middle East conflict is escalating and the situation is far from being under control. Anyway, in this article I want to talk less about geopolitics and more about technical analysis. In particular, I want to show you an indicator that is often overlooked but it has been pretty reliable over the past three decades.

The reason why I consider it reliable is not only that it was able to predict every recession over the last 29 years, but it has never provided a false positive signal. That’s rather uncommon, in fact, reliable indicators like Yield Curve Inversion, LEI indicator and Sahm Rule failed to be accurate in 2022-2023. They all predicted a recession in those years, and even though the stock market fell significantly, we didn’t experience the much-awaited recession. The indicator that I am going to show you didn’t fall in that trap. Here is how it is built:

  • It is a ratio. In the numerator we have the 3-Month Treasury Bill Yield.
  • In the denominator we have the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread. Simply put, it is the Junk Bond Spread, the interest rate paid by companies with a low credit rating minus the risk-free interest rate.

When this ratio goes up, it is a positive signal for the economy. It means that the Junk Bond Spread is narrowing, which is typical of a risk-on macroeconomic environment. It might also mean that the economy is growing fast, and to prevent an overheating, the Fed starts raising short-term interest rates. If interest rates rise faster than the widening Junk Bond Spread, then the ratio goes up anyway, so no recession on our way.

When the ratio goes down, that’s when the problems arise. It might be that Fed needs to cut rates immediately to face a severe economic stagnation, or it might mean that the Junk Bond Spread is widening, and that’s a sign that the risk-on environment is fading away. In some cases, both things can happen, creating a violent crash in the indicator. With that said, here is its chart from 1997 to the present (I didn’t find any data before this date).

As you can see, every time the ratio dropped and significantly broke below the 100-period weekly moving average, there was a recession (red areas) over the following months. In 2022 there was no false positive signal. In addition, here is an interesting fact that it is visible if we compare it to the S&P500.

As you can notice, this indicator doesn’t tell you that a recession is coming when it falls, but when it falls and then rebounds. The rebound has always hit the 100-period moving average or at least it has never surpassed it, and that was the signal of an imminent recession.

We had our rebound following the Liberation Day. If tariffs hadn’t been reduced, chances are that we could have faced a recession. But tariffs were lowered, and the risk-on environment took the lead for a while, until the labor market started to show some cracks in late 2025. The current energy crisis might be the final blow to bring this ratio way down into recession territory.

Of course, this is not a certainty and every indicator might signal a false positive, after all, it happened multiple times over the last years. However, if this indicator will be accurate once again, I don’t expect a violent crash as in 2008 and 2020. If you notice, the current trend reminds us much more what happened in 2000, so a gradual and slow downfall. Peak to bottom, it took the market 943 days to discount the entire consequences of the dotcom bubble, much more than the 517 days during the subprime crisis.

Regardless of the shape of the current trend, there is also a fundamental reason that makes me believe that this ratio won't fall violently, which is the limited downside/upside of the numerator. In fact, even though the current energy crisis will likely lead to a higher inflation rate, the Fed doesn’t have much room for maneuver. It can’t lower rates since it would further fuel the inflation rate, but it can’t even increase them as the labor market is weaker than 2022.

So, even if the economy slows down, the Fed Funds Rate could remain stationary; this means that it all depends on the Junk Bond Spread. In other words, how low credit rating companies are going to bear a severe energy crisis. Typically, they are very vulnerable. When you already have distressed financials, a global energy crisis is the last thing you need.

Conclusion

The perfect indicator that predicts every recession doesn’t exist, but the one I showed you in this article has been rather reliable over the past three decades. It is signaling a potential recession over the next months, in fact, it broke below the 100-period moving average and then bounced back. Now we are in the descending phase after that bounce, the most dangerous one.

We’ll see how things evolve, but over the previous recessions, the bottom has always been reached once the indicator was below the 0.15% threshold. We are now at 1.12%, so very far from it.

data.title: Oracle: Building Immunity To 'SaaSpocalypse' As It Lives Up To Its AI Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My last article on Oracle Corporation (ORCL) in December 2025 was an Earnings preview where I argued that investors should focus on management’s clarity on AI infrastructure funding, any clarity on the ability of the company to fulfil its debt obligations, and the company’s guidance on margins. I had a Hold rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, ORCL’s stock has been on a free fall, tumbling 30.13%, significantly underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which fell 4.2%.

ORCL’s steep decline can be attributed to a combination of factors, including macro headwinds, the so-called “SaaSpocalypse,” and growing investor concern over the company’s aggressive $45-50 billion financing plan for 2026.

ORCL recently reported its Q3 numbers and despite an impressive report and positive management commentary, which focused on assuaging concerns over both its debt obligations and the impact from “SaaSpocalypse,” the stock has failed to gain traction despite gaining immediately post the earnings release.

The question then becomes, are the concerns still real despite the company positing solid numbers? Or has ORCL become attractive at current levels?

In this article, I analyze the company’s fiscal Q3 report with an emphasis on the pace at which the company is converting its astounding RPOs into revenues as well as how the company is navigating the concerns surrounding “Saaspocalypse.” I also deploy an upgraded ensemble valuation model, involving machine learning models, to identify ORCL’s current valuation.

A Snapshot of Oracle’s Q3 Results

ORCL had a strong quarter, with a comfortable beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. More specifically, Q3 revenues came in at $17.19 billion, up 21.7% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $281.1 million. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.79, up 21% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.10. Cloud infrastructure saw yet another quarter of strong growth, with revenues up 81% y/y on a constant-currency basis. Non-GAAP operating margins came in at 43%, a decline of 100 bps y/y.

The company’s guidance was also strong. For Q4, management now expects revenues to come in between $18.93 billion and $19.24 billion, surpassing the analyst estimates of $19.11 billion. Adjusted earnings are expected to come in the range between $1.92 and $1.96, also coming in above the analyst consensus of $1.93.

RPO Growth Continues Its Impressive Trajectory, But Q3 Shows Conversion to Revenue Gaining Momentum

In my last earnings analysis on ORCL, I stressed upon the astounding growth levels of the company’s Remaining Performance Obligations [RPOs], which touched $455 billion in Q1, a y/y growth of 359%. Q3 showed that the RPO growth shows no signs of losing steam, as evidenced by the y/y growth of 325% and coming in at $553 billion.

Crucially, Q3 also demonstrated that the RPO conversion is gaining steam, which is what really matters for investors. Given the substantial capex spending that has been undertaken by the company in recent quarters, it was vital that the backlog was being converted into actual revenue. From that perspective, the company’s performance was impressive. More specifically, Q3 was the first quarter in over 15 years where both organic total revenue and non-GAAP EPS grew more than 20% in constant currency. This milestone alone should alleviate any investor concerns surrounding the ability of ORCL to convert its RPOs to revenues.

Furthermore, during the earnings call, management clarified the company’s roadmap for converting the $553 billion in RPOs into future revenues. More specifically, they announced that ORCL has now secured more than 10 GW of data center capacity, which is estimated to come online over the next three years. More importantly, 90% of this capacity would be fully funded through its partners and not by the company itself, which should ease any concerns about ORCL’s future debt levels. In Q3, ORCL delivered more than 90% of its committed capacity on or ahead of schedule. Finally, management also announced that the time from rack delivery to revenue has been reduced by 60% in the past several months.

Yet another development that supports the thesis that the RPO-to-revenues conversion is gaining steam is the company’s FY27 revenue guidance, which has been raised to $90 billion. Overall, Q3 demonstrated how ORCL’s status is transforming from one that promises cloud-driven growth to one that delivers on the promise, quarter after quarter.

The Market Needs to Pay More Attention to ORCL’s Multicloud Database Segment

One of the other key takeaways from ORCL’s Q3 report is the strong growth seen in the company’s Multicloud database revenues. More specifically, the segment saw its revenue jump a whopping 531% y/y, with the rapid expansion of the company’s partnerships with the likes of Microsoft (MSFT) [live in 33 regions], Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) [live in 14 regions], and Amazon (AMZN) [live in 8 regions, with expectations of being live in 22 regions by end of Q4], being the primary catalyst.

From a financial perspective, the multicloud architecture remains significant. As multicloud database revenues scale, the future margin profile of the overall cloud segment is expected to significantly improve given that the gross margins of database services are in the range of 60-80% compared to 30-40% gross margins for the AI cloud infrastructure. This demonstrates how ORCL’s multicloud database segment could be a multi-billion dollar contributor in the medium-to-long term.

The acceleration in the multicloud segment signals that the AI revolution and the AI-related spending remain intact. The ability of ORCL’s multicloud architecture to seamlessly enable enterprises to co-locate their most valuable private data with their respective AI agents on the cloud is, in my opinion, an underappreciated catalyst.

ORCL is Unlikely to be a Victim of the “SaaSpocalypse”

Finally, the investment thesis of ORCL would not be complete without understanding the impact of the so-called “SaaSpocalypse” on ORCL. Analysts did question this impact during the earnings call, and it was immediately dismissed by the management, who argued that the company is indeed relying on the AI coding tools to build more software at a faster pace and with smaller engineering teams.

If one looks at the Q3 report closely, then there is enough evidence to back management’s claim. For instance, Q3 revenues from cloud applications jumped 11% y/y to achieve a $16.1 billion annualized run rate. In addition, Fusion ERP was up 14%, Fusion SCM and HCM were both up 15% and Industry SaaS solutions were up 19%. Crucially, as a direct impact of AI-powered implementation tools, ORCL saw more than 2,000 of its customers go live in Q3 at a much faster rate than before. Furthermore, towards the end of last month, ORCL also initiated another round of layoffs across its SaaS and NetSuite segments, attributing the layoffs to the AI coding tools.

Putting it all together, AI is clearly acting as a catalyst to ORCL’s applications suite and not as a deterrent. Having said that, it is vital that investors pay close attention to any future impact of layoffs on the execution risk in the company’s applications business and whether the layoffs are indeed a result of the AI coding tools or if it’s a direct result of the potential dilution and execution risk stemming from its aggressive plans to raise $45-50 billion in 2026, of which the at-the-market equity portion still remains to be resolved. The debt load and the financing risk is yet another reason why investors should monitor any future adverse impact from “SaaSpocalypse” on ORCL.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$183.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

25x

PV of Projected FY26 EPS

$7.39

Source: Author’s Ensemble Model.

My price target for ORCL was calculated using the forward P/E multiple approach. I replaced my single ARIMA model, which I used last time to calculate the projected EPS, with a cloud-transition stacking ensemble model. More specifically, the ensemble model combines a momentum-decay model with two gradient-boosted machine learning models [XGBoost and LightGBM]. All of these models have been explained in detail in my previous article on (AVGO). The models were combined using a dynamic weighting layer, which ensures that higher weights are automatically allocated to those models that have a lower forecast error.

I would also like to mention here that the ensemble model initially had a fourth component, which was an Elastic Net model [a regularized linear model]. The purpose of including this model was to see if it can capitalize on ORCL’s smoother, linear earnings trajectory. However, during model training, the results of the Elastic Net model showed a constant growth prediction. This confirmed that ORCL’s EPS dynamics are actually non-linear and therefore better captured by tree-based machine learning models. As a result, the Elastic net was removed from the ensemble, leaving the ensemble with the aforementioned three models.

Similar to the process of computing AVGO’s projected EPS in my last article, all three machine learning models were trained on a feature set that included 12 predictive signals across three categories:

  1. Momentum – EPS Growth (q/q and y/y), Growth Acceleration, Fiscal Quarter Seasonality
  2. Fundamentals – Revenue Growth, Operating Margin Trend, Receivables Growth that acts as a proxy for RPOs, Earnings Stability
  3. Macroeconomic signals – Fed Funds rate, 10-year T-yield, Retail Sales growth, Enterprise Software Spending.

The models were trained on a peer group of 6 companies, which included both enterprise software and cloud infrastructure companies. The peer group consisted of ORCL, Salesforce (CRM), SAP (SAP), IBM (IBM), Adobe (ADBE), and ServiceNow (NOW).

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for all the models to determine their accuracy. More specifically, the forecasting accuracy was determined over 12 distinct windows (last three years). On each occasion, the model was trained only on the data prior to a specific quarter and was subsequently asked to predict the following four quarters. Critically, data leakage was eliminated by re-fitting the data normalization inside each fold using only historically available data, which ensures that the reported error metrics are genuinely out-of-sample.

The model evaluation metrics are as follows:

LightGBM

Before proceeding further, it is important for investors to understand that ORCL’s MAPE is significantly higher compared to the 5.29% MAPE achieved on the ensemble model with the same constituents for AVGO. The takeaway from this discrepancy is that it is more difficult to forecast an enterprise software company that is currently midway through its cloud transition, compared to a semiconductor company that is clearly benefiting from a more established AI demand curve. Furthermore, ORCL’s fiscal-quarter seasonality (Q4 is when there is a spike in license deals, Q1 is when this spike dissipates) results in systematic sequential swings, which lead to elevated forecast errors. This uncertainty is properly captured by the conformal prediction intervals, which is displayed later.

To further improve the predecessor model, I deployed an inverse-MAPE weighting scheme instead of manually assigning fixed weights. The approach automatically allocates a higher ensemble weight to those models with a lower forecast error.

The resulting allocation was:

  1. XGBoost – 40.5% (dominant weight of XGBoost implies that the model is more accurate relative to other models)
  2. LightGBM – 29.8%
  3. Momentum-Decay – 29.7%.

The projected EPS for the next four quarters, as predicted by the ensemble model, is as follows:

The ensemble model’s forward Q4 EPS of $7.39 is higher than the previously projected EPS of $6.16 in my previous article on ORCL. This can be attributed to two factors:

  1. ORCL’s actual Q3 FY26 EPS of $1.79 was confirmation of the acceleration in earnings as a result of its transition to the cloud.
  2. Relative to a single ARIMA model, the upgraded ensemble model managed to effectively capture both the growth acceleration and the earnings stability signals.

It is important to note here that the forecasts from individual models exhibit meaningful divergence, which is considered to be a healthy sign for an ensemble. The ensemble model blends the perspectives from a more bullish model [XGBoost] and a more conservative model [LightGBM], which subsequently results in an overall forecast that is more robust than any individual model.

I also deployed conformal prediction intervals to quantify the uncertainty around the point estimate of the ensemble model. These also help to determine the projected EPS in the bear and bull case scenarios. The conformal prediction intervals further confirm the wider uncertainty captured by the model MAPEs as I mentioned earlier.
